schema_notes: First Rashidun caliph. Closest companion of the Prophet, his father-in-law. Journey follows him from pre-Islamic Meccan merchant through Hijra, battles, the crisis after the Prophet's death, and the Ridda Wars (some of which he led in person).
sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (Guillaume tr.)
Wilferd Madelung, The Succession to Muhammad

journey.event: Born in Mecca to the Banu Taym clan of Quraysh. His father was Abu Quhafa. Grew up to become a successful cloth merchant.
Became a respected Meccan merchant known for integrity. A close friend of Muhammad years before the revelation; the two were nearly the same age.

Accepted Islam within days of the first revelation. Tradition names him the first free adult male to believe. His wealth and standing brought many early converts — Uthman, Zubayr, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas — into Islam through him.
During the years of Meccan persecution, used his wealth to buy and free enslaved Muslims, including Bilal ibn Rabah. Suffered public beatings himself for defending the Prophet.
Publicly affirmed the Prophet's account of the Isra and Mi'raj without hesitation, earning the title As-Siddiq, 'the Truthful One' — the name history would remember him by.
Chosen by the Prophet to be his sole companion on the Hijra. Slipped out of Mecca with him by night as Quraysh assassins closed in.
Hid three nights with the Prophet in the Cave of Thawr south of Mecca while search parties passed by the mouth. Moments the Quran would later recall: 'the second of two, when they were in the cave'.
Arrived with the Prophet at Quba on the outskirts of Yathrib after an eight-day desert journey.
Entered Medina and settled in the new Muslim community. Gave his daughter A'isha in marriage to the Prophet.
Fought at the Battle of Badr as one of the Prophet's closest lieutenants. His own son Abd al-Rahman fought on the Quraysh side that day.
Present at Uhud. Stood firm with the small group who rallied around the wounded Prophet when the line broke.
Fought at the Battle of the Trench during the month-long siege of Medina by the Confederates.
Accompanied the Prophet on the pilgrimage attempt halted at Hudaybiyyah. Among the very first to give the Pledge of the Tree (Bay'at al-Ridwan).
Entered Mecca with the Prophet at the bloodless conquest. His aged father Abu Quhafa accepted Islam on the same day.
Fought at Hunayn. Famously said, as the huge Muslim army marched out, 'We shall not this day be worsted by reason of the smallness of our numbers' — a remark the Quran is said to have reproved.
Present at the siege of Ta'if.
Accompanied the Prophet on the Tabuk expedition, the last of the Prophet's campaigns. Gave all his wealth to finance the army, keeping nothing back.
Led the Hajj of the year 9 AH on the Prophet's behalf — the first Islamic Hajj. Ali caught up with him on the route carrying the newly-revealed Surah al-Tawbah to proclaim.
Performed the Farewell Pilgrimage at the Prophet's side.

Led the prayers in Medina during the Prophet's final illness. On the Prophet's death, after the meeting at the Saqifa of Banu Sa'ida, was acknowledged as the first caliph — the Successor to the Messenger of God.
Personally marched from Medina to confront the Ridda (apostasy) rebels at Dhu Qissa, about 24 miles east of the city. Defeated the rebels on 1 August 632 and captured the position.

Moved the army on to Abraq (some 72 miles northeast of Medina) where the retreating tribes had gathered. Defeated them there, then returned to Medina to direct the broader Ridda campaign through eleven commanders sent across Arabia.
Directed the Ridda Wars from Medina: Khalid ibn al-Walid to Yamama against Musaylima, others to Oman, Bahrain, Yemen, Hadramaut. Within one year the Arabian peninsula was reunified.
Ordered the compilation of the Quran into a single mushaf after the Battle of Yamama killed many of the memorisers. The task was given to Zayd ibn Thabit.
Launched the northern campaigns: Khalid ibn al-Walid sent into Iraq, and forces dispatched against Byzantine Syria. He himself remained in Medina.

Fell ill in Medina. Before dying, nominated Umar ibn al-Khattab as his successor to avoid a second succession crisis. Died on 22 or 23 August 634 (22 Jumada al-Thani 13 AH) after a caliphate of just over two years. Buried beside the Prophet in A'isha's chamber.
